nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky reefs and sand-weed areas at roughly 1–4 m depth; typically observed sheltering inside vacant mollusc tubes, vermetid snail tubes, and old barnacle shells on rocky outcrops along the eastern Pacific coast.

diet.notes: Has been reported to feed on small benthic invertebrates and zooplankton in the wild. In captivity it tends to accept finely chopped meaty preparations, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, and similar small-particle frozen foods. Best fed small portions multiple times per day so the reclusive fish gets a chance to dart out for food.

summary: A diminutive eastern Pacific tube blenny (family Chaenopsidae) that shelters head-out from vacated mollusc tubes and barnacle shells. Generally considered reef-safe and best suited to mature, rockwork-heavy aquaria.
careNotes:
The Eyebrow Barnacle Blenny (Ekemblemaria myersi), also known as the Reefsand Blenny, is a small tube blenny in the family Chaenopsidae native to the eastern central Pacific from the Gulf of California south to Colombia. In the wild it inhabits shallow rocky outcrops in sand-weed habitat at roughly 1–4 m depth, where it occupies vacated mollusc tubes, vermetid snail tubes, and old barnacle shells, generally exposing only its head.

Note: LiveAquaria's product page lists the family as 'Antennariidae' (frogfishes), which is an error — multiple taxonomic references (FishBase, Wikipedia, Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes) place Ekemblemaria in the Chaenopsidae. The Chaenopsidae assignment is used here.

It is generally regarded as reef-safe and well suited to mature reef tanks with a great deal of fine rockwork; the fish is highly reclusive and tends to spend most of the day partially concealed inside a tube or crevice, darting out to seize passing food. A well-established system with many small holes, vermetid tubes, and barnacle shells will give it places to feel secure. Sand-bed and rubble zones are commonly advised.

Most sources describe it as peaceful toward unrelated fish but semi-aggressive in defence of its chosen tube; it is best kept singly unless the tank is unusually large and offers multiple well-separated tube territories. Pairing it with boisterous, fast-feeding tankmates is commonly cautioned against because food can be intercepted before this small, hesitant feeder leaves cover.

Sources disagreed on tank size: LiveAquaria lists 30 US gal (~114 L). Per the playbook's tank-sizing rule a cautious round-up to 120 L is used here, which gives a small reclusive fish ample rockwork without overstating requirements. Temperature ranges also disagreed slightly — LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C versus FishBase preferred range 23.3–29.1°C; an averaged 22–27°C is used to span both. Adult size was reported as 'up to 2.75 in' (~7 cm) by LiveAquaria and 7.0 cm TL by FishBase; 5–7 cm is used to capture typical aquarium specimens up to the species maximum.

Like many small blennies it can be a jumper; a tightly fitting lid is commonly advised. Coloration is dark brown to black with 10–12 darker bars and a prominent ocellated gill-cover (operculum) spot.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of husbandry parameters: pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), temperature 72–78°F, dKH 8–12, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe, carnivore diet, 30 gal minimum (~114 L), max ~2.75 in (~7 cm), reclusive behavior. Note: page lists family as 'Antennariidae', which is incorrect (Chaenopsidae is the accepted family per FishBase and Wikipedia).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: fishbase.org returned HTTP 403; the .se mirror was used. Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaenopsidae, Blenniiformes), maximum length (7.0 cm TL), distribution (Gulf of California to Colombia), depth range (1–4 m), habitat (mollusc tubes on rocky outcrops in sand-weed areas), preferred temperature (23.3–29.1°C, mean 27.4°C), diet (invertebrates), trophic level 3.5, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Ekemblemaria myersi)
additional.notes: Confirmed family Chaenopsidae, order Blenniiformes, common name 'Reefsand blenny', range Gulf of California to Colombia, max length 7 cm, feeds on zoobenthos, described 1963 by J. S. Stephens, named after George S. Myers. Used for taxonomy fallback after LiveAquaria misclassified the family.
